name: Jollof Rice (Ghanaian)
description: Ghana's answer to the great West African jollof debate, cooked in a clay pot for a smoky depth, with a distinct tomato base and generous seasoning. Lighter than Nigerian jollof but no less celebrated.
instructions: Blend tomatoes, bell peppers, scotch bonnet and half the onion into a smooth paste.
Heat oil in a pot. Fry the blended pepper mix for 20 minutes until the oil rises to the top.
Add chicken stock, curry, thyme, bay leaves, and seasoning cubes.
Wash rice and add to the pot. Stir to coat in the sauce.
Add water so liquid sits 2cm above the rice.
Cover with foil then lid. Cook on low heat 35 minutes.
Uncover, stir from the bottom, serve.

name: Injera and Doro Wat
description: Ethiopia's national dish, a deeply spiced, slow-cooked chicken stew (Doro Wat) made with niter kibbeh and berbere spice, served on a bed of spongy sourdough injera flatbread.
ingredients: 4 tablespoons niter kibbeh (spiced clarified butter) or regular butter
instructions: Cook the red onions in a dry pot on very low heat, stirring constantly for 30 minutes until deeply caramelised without oil, this is the foundation.
Add the niter kibbeh and berbere spice. Fry for 10 minutes until dark and fragrant.
Add garlic and ginger. Fry 3 minutes more.
Add the chicken pieces. Brown on all sides.
Pour in the chicken stock. Bring to simmer.
Cook covered on low heat for 45 minutes until the chicken is very tender.
Add the whole hard-boiled eggs in the last 15 minutes.
Serve on injera, spooning the wat over the bread.

name: Ndizi na Nyama
description: A fragrant Swahili coast stew of unripe plantains and beef slow-cooked in a coconut milk and tomato broth. Warm spices from the Indian Ocean trade routes make this uniquely East African.
instructions: Season beef with salt and half the spices. Brown in oil for 5 minutes.
Add onion and garlic. Cook 3 minutes.
Add tomatoes, remaining spices, and cook until the tomatoes break down.
Add coconut milk and enough water to cover. Simmer 20 minutes.
Add the plantain pieces. Cook for 20 more minutes until both beef and plantain are tender.
Adjust seasoning. Garnish with fresh coriander.
Serve with rice or ugali.

name: Braai Boerewors
description: South Africa's beloved coiled sausage, seasoned with coriander, cloves, and nutmeg and grilled over hot charcoal at the iconic braai (barbecue). A South African institution.
instructions: Mix all meats and spices together thoroughly. Add vinegar and mix again.
Chill the mixture for 1 hour.
Fill the sausage casings, do not overfill. Coil the sausage as you go.
Grill over medium-hot charcoal, turning carefully to avoid breaking the coil.
Cook for 15–20 minutes until cooked through with char marks.
Serve on a bread roll with chakalaka relish or alongside pap (mealie pap).

name: Thieboudienne
description: Senegal's national dish and one of the great rice dishes of the world, fish and vegetables slow-cooked in a tomato-based broth, then the rice is cooked in that same rich stock.
ingredients: 3 cups broken rice or parboiled rice
400g mixed vegetables (cassava, carrot, cabbage, aubergine, sweet potato)
Rof stuffing (parsley, garlic, scotch bonnet, blended)
instructions: Make rof by blending parsley, garlic, and scotch bonnet. Stuff into slits in the fish.
Brown the fish in oil on both sides. Remove and set aside.
In the same oil, fry the blended tomatoes and onion for 20 minutes.
Add all the vegetables and enough water to create a broth. Season well.
Cook vegetables until nearly done, about 20 minutes.
Remove fish and vegetables. Keep the stock.
Cook the rice in the tomato-fish stock until absorbed.
Plate the rice, top with the fish and vegetables arranged decoratively.

name: Ugali and Sukuma Wiki
description: East Africa's daily staple, stiff, smooth maize meal paired with sukuma wiki (kale stir-fried with tomatoes and onions). Simple, nutritious, and eaten across Kenya, Tanzania, and Uganda.
instructions: Bring water to boil in a pot. Gradually add maize flour, stirring constantly.
Stir vigorously for 5 minutes over medium heat until very thick and smooth.
Cover and cook on low for 10 minutes, stirring every 3 minutes.
For sukuma wiki: heat oil, fry onion for 3 minutes, add tomatoes and cook 5 minutes.
Add the shredded kale, stir fry for 5–7 minutes. Season with salt.
Serve ugali moulded into a dome alongside the sukuma wiki.

name: Tagine
description: A fragrant Moroccan slow-cooked lamb stew with preserved lemon, olives, and ras el hanout spice blend. Named after the conical clay pot it is cooked in.
instructions: Toss lamb in ras el hanout, turmeric, cinnamon, and salt.
Brown in olive oil in a tagine or heavy pot. Remove.
Fry onions and garlic in the same pot for 5 minutes.
Return lamb, add tinned tomatoes and enough water to partially cover.
Cover and cook on the lowest heat for 90 minutes.
Add preserved lemon and olives in the last 20 minutes.
Garnish with fresh coriander. Serve with couscous or flatbread.

name: Fufu and Light Soup
description: Ghana's most beloved comfort food, fresh cassava and plantain pounded into smooth, elastic fufu balls served in a clear, spicy palm nut or chicken light soup.
instructions: For fufu: pound boiled cassava and plantain together in a mortar until completely smooth and elastic, wetting hands regularly.
For soup: blend tomatoes, peppers, onion, and ginger.
Cook meat in seasoned water until tender. Add the blended tomatoes.
Simmer for 20 minutes. Season with salt and seasoning cubes.
Adjust the soup to be light and brothy, not thick.
Mould fufu into smooth balls and place in deep bowls.
Ladle the light soup over the fufu to serve.

name: Chakalaka
description: A fiery, tangy South African vegetable relish made with onions, peppers, carrots, and baked beans. A staple side dish at braais, served with pap or bread.
instructions: Heat oil in a pan. Fry onion until soft.
Add curry powder and paprika. Fry 1 minute.
Add grated carrots and diced peppers. Cook 5 minutes.
Add tomatoes and scotch bonnet. Cook 5 minutes until soft.
Add the baked beans. Stir and cook 5 more minutes.
Season with salt and pepper.
Serve hot or cold alongside pap, boerewors, or bread.

name: Kelewele
description: Ghana's irresistible street food, ripe plantain cubes marinated in ginger, pepper, and spices then deep-fried until caramelised and spicy. Sold everywhere after dark.
instructions: Peel plantains and cut into cubes or chunks.
Mix ginger, pepper, nutmeg, and salt. Toss the plantain in the spice mixture.
Leave to marinate for 15 minutes.
Heat oil in a pot to medium-high.
Fry the plantain in batches for 4–5 minutes until deep golden and caramelised.
Drain on paper towels. Serve hot.
